title: Semantic Renormalization Group
subtitle: Operators as Fixed Points of Meaning Coarse-Graining

children.children: Schankian primitives are RG fixed points of a semantic coarse-graining process. Different surface event descriptions flow to the same fixed-point operator under progressive detail removal — just as different physical systems share universality classes.

props.title: 1. The Core Insight: Zoom Out Until Structure Appears

children.children: In physics, the Renormalization Group (RG) asks: what happens when you systematically remove fine-grained details? As you zoom out, many different microscopic systems flow to the same macroscopic behavior. Water boiling and magnets demagnetizing look nothing alike up close, but their critical behavior is governed by the same mathematics.

pairs: Progressive detail removal from event descriptions

children.children: The claim: Schankian primitives are what SURVIVES when you progressively strip away surface details from event descriptions. 'Chop parsley', 'tear letter', 'split road' — remove the instrument, manner, and specific entities, and what remains is the same fixed-point operator: SEPARATE.

props.title: What is the Renormalization Group, really?

children.children: The RG isn't a single group in the mathematical sense — it's a semi-group of transformations that map a system's description at one scale to its description at a coarser scale. Kenneth Wilson won the Nobel Prize in 1982 for showing how this works.

The key steps: (1) Integrate out fine-grained degrees of freedom (block spin transformation). (2) Rescale to restore the original resolution. (3) Repeat. Systems that flow to the same fixed point share a 'universality class' — they have identical behavior at large scales despite different microscopic details.

In our semantic analogy: (1) Remove a detail from the event description (instrument? manner? specific entity?). (2) Ask: is the core meaning preserved? (3) Repeat until only the primitive remains. Events that flow to the same primitive share a 'semantic universality class.'
Let's trace how different surface descriptions flow to the same fixed point through progressive coarse-graining:

steps.label: Surface Level
steps.description: Full event descriptions with all details

items.chop: The chef rapidly chopped the parsley with a sharp knife on the cutting board
items.tear: Maria angrily tore the letter into pieces with her bare hands
items.split: The earthquake slowly split the road along a 2-mile crack
items.dice: The sous-chef carefully diced the onion into quarter-inch cubes

items.chop: The chef chopped the parsley with a knife on the cutting board
items.tear: Maria tore the letter into pieces with her hands
items.split: The earthquake split the road along a crack
items.dice: The sous-chef diced the onion into cubes

items.tear: Maria tore the letter into pieces

steps.label: Remove Agent Details
steps.description: Replace specific agents with generic roles

items.chop: Someone separated the parsley into parts
items.tear: Someone separated the letter into parts
items.split: Something separated the road into parts
items.dice: Someone separated the onion into parts

steps.label: Fixed Point ⭐
steps.description: The Schankian primitive — all four converge

items.chop: SEPARATE(agent, patient) → patient is in pieces
items.tear: SEPARATE(agent, patient) → patient is in pieces
items.split: SEPARATE(agent, patient) → patient is in pieces
items.dice: SEPARATE(agent, patient) → patient is in pieces

children.children: Notice: at the fixed point, all four descriptions are IDENTICAL. The surface differences (chop/tear/split/dice) were 'irrelevant operators' in RG language — they vanish under coarse-graining. What remains (SEPARATE) is a 'relevant operator' that persists at all scales.

Here's the deepest implication: if Schankian operators are RG fixed points, then different languages are different microscopic systems flowing to the SAME fixed points. English 'give', German 'geben', Mandarin '给' (gěi), Swahili '-pa' — all flow to ATRANS under semantic coarse-graining.

items.definition: A set of systems that flow to the same RG fixed point. In our framework: all surface verbs across all languages that describe the same primitive state change.
A perturbation that grows under RG flow — it matters at all scales. For us: the core state transformation (SEPARATE, TRANSFER, HARM, etc.).
A perturbation that shrinks under RG flow — it only matters at fine scales. For us: manner, instrument, style, specific entity details.
A perturbation that neither grows nor shrinks — it stays the same at all scales. Possibly: polarity (positive/negative) and volitionality (intentional/accidental).

children.children: The RG framework doesn't just classify — it makes quantitative predictions. In physics, systems near a fixed point obey power-law scaling. Can we find semantic scaling laws?

props.title: Prediction: Semantic Scaling Near Fixed Points

children.children: Consider how quickly different details become 'irrelevant' under coarse-graining:
Instrument details vanish fastest — 'with a knife' vs 'with scissors' is the first distinction lost
Manner details go next — 'rapidly' vs 'slowly' stops mattering
Agent details persist longer — WHO did it matters more than HOW
The state change persists indefinitely — it's the fixed point

This ordering predicts a semantic scaling hierarchy: details with larger 'scaling exponents' are more relevant to the core operator. This is testable! Measure how much each detail contributes to predicting the next event in a narrative — that's the empirical scaling exponent.

props.title: Critical Exponents in Physics vs Semantics

children.children: In physics, critical exponents describe how quantities scale near phase transitions. The correlation length diverges as ξ ~ |T - Tc|^(-ν). The magnetization vanishes as M ~ |T - Tc|^β. Different physical systems with the same exponents are in the same universality class.

The semantic analogy: as we progressively coarse-grain event descriptions, the 'semantic correlation length' (how far ahead you can predict in a narrative) should scale predictably. Events near an operator boundary (ambiguous between HARM and HELP, say) should show critical-like behavior — slower convergence, larger fluctuations, more sensitivity to context.
How do we actually implement semantic RG? Here's a concrete procedure:

pipeline.detail: Map event descriptions to vectors using a sentence encoder (e.g., Sentence-BERT). Each description becomes a point in high-dimensional space.
Group nearby event vectors (block spin transformation). 'Chop parsley' and 'dice onion' land in the same block. Use clustering (k-means, HDBSCAN) or learned grouping.
Replace each block with its centroid. This is the coarse-grained description — a 'block event' that captures shared structure while discarding within-block variation.
Normalize the coarse-grained vectors to restore the original scale. This is analogous to rescaling after block spin transformation.
Repeat steps 2-4 at the coarser level. Each iteration removes another layer of detail. Track which descriptions merge at each step — that's the RG flow.
When further coarse-graining doesn't change the representation, you've found a fixed point. That's your Schankian operator.

children.children: This procedure requires choosing a coarse-graining scale at each step. Too aggressive → you merge HARM and HELP into a single 'AFFECT' blob. Too conservative → you never reach fixed points. The MDL principle (Entry 45) provides the stopping criterion: stop when further coarse-graining increases description length.
The deepest payoff: RG explains WHY the same primitives appear across unrelated languages, without requiring historical contact or shared ancestry.

pairs: Water and iron have completely different microscopic physics
English and Mandarin have completely different grammars
Both show the same critical exponents (Ising universality class)
Both converge to the same semantic primitives (ATRANS, PROPEL, etc.)
It requires the same symmetry structure and dimensionality
It requires the same event structure and state-change physics
The RG flow is deterministic given the symmetry class
The coarse-graining flow is deterministic given event physics

children.children: In other words: every language needs to describe the same physical world. The world has a finite number of qualitatively different state-change types. Any language's verbs, when coarse-grained, must converge to these types. Schankian operators are universal because the WORLD is universal — different languages are just different microscopic encodings of the same macroscopic physics.

props.title: Wierzbicka's Natural Semantic Metalanguage as Evidence

children.children: Anna Wierzbicka's NSM project identified ~65 semantic primes that appear to be lexicalized in EVERY human language: DO, HAPPEN, MOVE, TOUCH, FEEL, THINK, KNOW, WANT, SAY, etc. These primes are strikingly similar to Schankian operators.

From an RG perspective, NSM primes ARE the fixed points of semantic coarse-graining, discovered empirically across 30+ typologically diverse languages. The RG framework explains why they exist and predicts their structure — they're not arbitrary, they're the mathematical inevitability of coarse-graining a shared physical world.
The RG perspective unifies several other entries in this brainstorm series:

items.definition: The IB curve IS the RG flow. Compression parameter β = RG scale. IB fixed points = RG fixed points. They're literally the same mathematics applied to information theory instead of statistical mechanics.
MDL provides the stopping criterion for RG flow. Stop coarse-graining when further compression increases description length. The MDL-optimal operator set = the set of RG fixed points.

Different frequency bands = different RG scales. Gamma (fine-grained processing) → Beta (intermediate) → Alpha/Theta (coarse-grained, schematic). The brain may implement semantic RG across oscillatory timescales.
Eigenoperators of the RG transformation = the operators that don't change under coarse-graining. The eigenvalues determine whether an operator is relevant (>1), irrelevant (<1), or marginal (=1).

children.children: The RG framework makes specific, falsifiable predictions:

predictions.claim: Hierarchical clustering of verb embeddings should show discrete convergence points
predictions.test: Apply hierarchical agglomerative clustering to Sentence-BERT embeddings of 10,000 event descriptions. Measure the dendrogram gap structure — there should be natural 'shelves' corresponding to operator-level abstractions, not a smooth hierarchy.

predictions.claim: Cross-lingual verb clusters should converge to the same fixed points
predictions.test: Apply the coarse-graining procedure independently to English, Mandarin, Arabic, and Swahili verb descriptions. Align the resulting fixed-point sets. Prediction: >80% overlap despite no shared training.

predictions.claim: Narrative prediction accuracy should be scale-invariant near fixed points
predictions.test: Replace verbs in narratives with their operator labels at different coarse-graining levels. Measure next-event prediction accuracy. Near the fixed point, accuracy should plateau — further coarse-graining doesn't help or hurt.

predictions.claim: Verbs near operator boundaries should show critical slowing
predictions.test: Identify verbs that are ambiguous between two operators (e.g., 'push' between PROPEL and MOVE). These should show longer processing times in psycholinguistic experiments and higher perplexity in LLMs.

children.children: The RG perspective reframes the entire Schankian operators project. We're not arbitrarily choosing primitives. We're not learning them from scratch. We're discovering the mathematical fixed points that ANY sufficiently rich description of events MUST converge to. The primitives are as inevitable as the Ising universality class.

The deepest claim: Schankian operators are not a theory ABOUT language. They're a theorem about the structure of event descriptions under coarse-graining. Any system that represents events — whether human language, LLM embeddings, or alien communication — must converge to the same fixed-point operators. They're as real as conservation laws.

This connects to Entry 31 (Semantic Conservation Laws): if operators are RG fixed points, then the symmetries they preserve under coarse-graining imply conservation laws via Noether's theorem. Possession is conserved under ATRANS. Causal responsibility is conserved under CAUSE. These aren't metaphors — they're mathematical consequences of the RG framework.

questions.question: In the RG framework, what does a 'fixed point' correspond to in our semantic theory?
options: A specific surface verb like 'chop'
A Schankian primitive operator like SEPARATE
The embedding of a particular entity
The learning rate of the training procedure
questions.explanation: Fixed points are what remains after all surface details have been coarse-grained away. In our framework, these are the Schankian primitive operators — the irreducible state-change types that survive progressive abstraction.

questions.question: Why does the RG framework predict cross-linguistic universality of semantic primitives?
options: Because all languages descended from a common ancestor
Because linguists defined them to be universal
Because all languages describe the same physical world, so coarse-graining converges to the same fixed points
Because neural networks always learn the same features
questions.explanation: Different languages are different 'microscopic' encodings of the same world. Under coarse-graining, they must converge to the same fixed points because the underlying event physics is universal — just as water and iron converge to the same critical behavior despite different atomic structures.

questions.question: What is an 'irrelevant operator' in the semantic RG framework?
options: An operator that doesn't exist in any language
A detail (like instrument or manner) that vanishes under coarse-graining
A Schankian primitive that we can safely ignore
An operator with a scaling exponent greater than 1
questions.explanation: Irrelevant operators are perturbations that shrink under RG flow — they don't affect the fixed-point behavior. In semantics, these are details like the specific instrument used ('with a knife' vs 'with scissors') or manner ('rapidly' vs 'carefully') that vanish when you zoom out to the operator level.

questions.question: How does the Information Bottleneck (Entry 39) relate to semantic RG?
options: They're unrelated frameworks that happen to use similar math
IB is a special case of RG that only works for discrete data
The IB compression parameter β plays the same role as the RG scale — they're the same mathematics
RG is a special case of IB
questions.explanation: The IB curve and the RG flow are mathematically equivalent! The compression parameter β in IB corresponds to the coarse-graining scale in RG. IB fixed points are RG fixed points. This deep connection means techniques from one framework directly transfer to the other.

questions.question: What provides the stopping criterion for semantic RG — how do you know when you've found the right level of abstraction?
options: Stop when you have exactly 14 operators (Schank's original count)
Stop when further coarse-graining increases description length (MDL principle)
Stop after exactly 5 iterations
Stop when cross-validation error starts increasing
questions.explanation: The Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle provides a principled stopping criterion. Too few operators = overly long descriptions of events. Too many operators = bloated model with redundant primitives. The MDL sweet spot is the fixed-point set where the total description (model + data given model) is minimized.
